---
title: "First Waves"
description: "Act I of a beginner-friendly journey toward Borwein integrals."
date: 2026-06-04
categories: [mathematics, integrals, visualization]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: false
---

## The strange fractions that behave well

Some expressions look dangerous before we understand them.

For example,

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{x}
$$

seems to have a problem at \(x=0\), because the denominator becomes \(0\). But the numerator also becomes \(0\), and the two zeros cancel each other in a very precise way.

Using the Taylor expansion

$$
\sin(ax)
=
ax-\frac{a^3x^3}{6}+\frac{a^5x^5}{120}-\cdots,
$$

we get

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{x}
=
a-\frac{a^3x^2}{6}+\frac{a^5x^4}{120}-\cdots.
$$

So as \(x\to0\),

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{x}\to a.
$$

Even better, if we divide by \(a\) as well, we get the normalized version

$$
S_a(x)=\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}.
$$

Then

$$
S_a(x)\to1
\qquad
\text{as}
\qquad
x\to0.
$$

This normalized sine fraction will be our main character for the rest of Act I.

```{ojs}
// Core helper functions for all Act I widgets.

Plot = require("@observablehq/plot@0.6")

sineFrac = (x, a) => Math.abs(a * x) < 1e-8 ? 1 : Math.sin(a * x) / (a * x)

cosDiffHalf = (x, a) => {
  // E_a(x) = (1 - cos(ax))/(a x)^2, with removable value 1/2 at x = 0.
  const z = a * x;
  return Math.abs(z) < 1e-7 ? 0.5 : (1 - Math.cos(z)) / (z * z);
}

cosDiffUnit = (x, a) => 2 * cosDiffHalf(x, a)

trapIntegral = (f, R, N = 6000) => {
  const h = R / N;
  let total = 0.5 * (f(0) + f(R));
  for (let i = 1; i < N; i++) {
    total += f(i * h);
  }
  return total * h;
}

cumulativeIntegralData = (f, R, N = 5000, keep = 500) => {
  const h = R / N;
  const jump = Math.max(1, Math.floor(N / keep));
  let total = 0;
  let previous = f(0);
  const out = [{R: 0, area: 0}];

  for (let i = 1; i <= N; i++) {
    const x = i * h;
    const current = f(x);
    total += 0.5 * (previous + current) * h;
    previous = current;

    if (i % jump === 0 || i === N) {
      out.push({R: x, area: total});
    }
  }

  return out;
}

fmt = x => Number.isFinite(x) ? x.toFixed(8) : "not finite"
```

## The wobble and the squeeze

The function

$$
S_a(x)=\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
$$

has two competing forces inside it.

The numerator \(\sin(ax)\) creates the wobble.

The denominator \(ax\) creates the squeeze.

As \(x\) grows, the sine wave keeps oscillating, but the denominator slowly crushes the height of those oscillations toward \(0\).

```{ojs}
viewof a_wave = Inputs.range([0.2, 8], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})
```

```{ojs}
xs_wave = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => i * 40 / 999)

Plot.line(xs_wave, {
  x: d => d,
  y: d => sineFrac(d, a_wave)
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "sin(ax)/(ax)", domain: [-0.25, 1.05]},
  grid: true
})
```

The important thing to notice is not just that the curve wiggles. It wiggles with shrinking strength.

That shrinking strength is what allows many of the improper integrals in this article to make sense.

---

## The first integral

A famous classical result says

$$
\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin(ax)}{x}\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2},
\qquad a>0.
$$

Since

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
=
\frac1a\frac{\sin(ax)}{x},
$$

we immediately get

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a}.
}
$$

This is the first sign that the normalized sine fraction has a clean hidden structure.

But there is one subtlety: this is not ordinary positive area. The curve goes above and below the \(x\)-axis. The final answer comes from cancellation between positive and negative waves.

The next plot shows the accumulated area

$$
A(R)=\int_0^R \frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}\,dx.
$$

As \(R\) grows, \(A(R)\) wobbles, but it slowly settles toward the predicted limit.

```{ojs}
viewof a_area = Inputs.range([0.2, 5], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof R_area = Inputs.range([10, 160], {
  value: 70,
  step: 5,
  label: "maximum R shown"
})
```

```{ojs}
areaData = cumulativeIntegralData(
  x => sineFrac(x, a_area),
  R_area,
  7000,
  600
)

areaLimit = Math.PI / (2 * a_area)

Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "R"},
  y: {label: "accumulated area"},
  marks: [
    Plot.line(areaData, {x: "R", y: "area"}),
    Plot.ruleY([areaLimit])
  ],
  grid: true
})
```

For the chosen value of \(a\), the predicted limit is

```{ojs}
html`<b>${fmt(areaLimit)}</b>`
```

So the curve does not simply pile up area forever. Its later oscillations mostly cancel.

---

## The first cosine-difference cousin

Now consider another expression:

$$
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{x^2}.
$$

At first glance, it also looks dangerous at \(x=0\). But again, Taylor expansion saves us.

Since

$$
\cos(ax)
=
1-\frac{a^2x^2}{2}+\frac{a^4x^4}{24}-\cdots,
$$

we get

$$
1-\cos(ax)
=
\frac{a^2x^2}{2}-\frac{a^4x^4}{24}+\cdots.
$$

Therefore

$$
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{x^2}\to \frac{a^2}{2}.
$$

The normalized version is

$$
E_a(x)
=
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}.
$$

Then

$$
E_a(x)\to \frac12.
$$

If we want a version that starts at \(1\), just multiply by \(2\):

$$
T_a(x)
=
\frac{2(1-\cos(ax))}{(ax)^2}.
$$

Then

$$
T_a(0)=1.
$$

This \(T_a(x)\) is the cosine-difference cousin of the normalized sine fraction.

The two are connected by the identity

$$
T_a(x)
=
\left(
\frac{\sin\left(\frac{a x}{2}\right)}
{\frac{a x}{2}}
\right)^2.
$$

So this new-looking object is secretly made from the same normalized sine fraction, only squared and evaluated at half the scale.

```{ojs}
viewof a_cousin = Inputs.range([0.2, 8], {
  value: 2,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})
```

```{ojs}
cousinData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 40 / 999;
  return {
    x,
    sine: sineFrac(x, a_cousin),
    cousin: cosDiffUnit(x, a_cousin)
  };
})

Plot.plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "value"},
  marks: [
    Plot.line(cousinData, {x: "x", y: "sine"}),
    Plot.line(cousinData, {x: "x", y: "cousin"})
  ],
  grid: true
})
```

The two curves behave differently, but they belong to the same family of ideas: removable trouble at zero, decay at infinity, and oscillatory structure underneath.

---

## Integral of the cosine-difference cousin

A simple integral follows from the earlier sine integral:

$$
\int_0^\infty
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{x^2}\,dx
=
\frac{\pi a}{2}.
$$

Dividing by \(a^2\), we get

$$
\int_0^\infty
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a}.
$$

And for the unit-normalized cousin,

$$
T_a(x)=\frac{2(1-\cos(ax))}{(ax)^2},
$$

we get

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty T_a(x)\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{a}.
}
$$

This is already a clue that the cosine-difference cousin is not a separate universe. It is connected to the normalized sine fraction by a very simple identity.

---

## Combining two normalized sine fractions

Now we begin the real bridge toward Borwein-type integrals.

Instead of integrating one normalized sine fraction, let us multiply two:

$$
I(a,b)
=
\int_0^\infty
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
\frac{\sin(bx)}{bx}
\,dx.
$$

The result is surprisingly clean:

$$
\boxed{
I(a,b)=\frac{\pi}{2\max(a,b)}.
}
$$

In other words, the larger scale controls the answer.

If \(a\geq b\), then

$$
I(a,b)=\frac{\pi}{2a}.
$$

That means adding a smaller second factor does not change the value of the original integral. This is the first small version of the plateau phenomenon.

```{ojs}
viewof a_two = Inputs.range([0.5, 6], {
  value: 2,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof b_two = Inputs.range([0.1, 8], {
  value: 0.8,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "b"
})
```

```{ojs}
approxTwo = trapIntegral(
  x => sineFrac(x, a_two) * sineFrac(x, b_two),
  140,
  9000
)

exactTwo = Math.PI / (2 * Math.max(a_two, b_two))

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.6;">
  Numerical approximation over a large finite interval:
  <b>${fmt(approxTwo)}</b><br>
  Exact value:
  <b>${fmt(exactTwo)}</b><br>
  Formula:
  <code>π / (2 max(a,b))</code>
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
twoData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 60 / 999;
  return {
    x,
    y: sineFrac(x, a_two) * sineFrac(x, b_two)
  };
})

Plot.line(twoData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "product"},
  grid: true
})
```

Try fixing \(a=2\), then moving \(b\) from \(0.1\) up to \(2\). The exact value remains fixed at

$$
\frac{\pi}{4}.
$$

Once \(b\) passes \(2\), the larger scale changes, and the answer changes.

This is the first real hint of the mystery that will drive Act II.

---

## Combining two cosine-difference cousins

Now let us do the same thing with

$$
E_a(x)=\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}.
$$

Consider

$$
K(a,b)=
\int_0^\infty
E_a(x)E_b(x)\,dx.
$$

Let

$$
m=\min(a,b),
\qquad
M=\max(a,b).
$$

Then

$$
\boxed{
K(a,b)
=
\frac{\pi m^2(3M-m)}{12a^2b^2}.
}
$$

This looks more complicated than the two-sine-fraction formula, but it still has a clean piecewise structure.

If \(a\geq b\), this simplifies to

$$
K(a,b)
=
\frac{\pi}{4}
\left(
\frac1a-\frac{b}{3a^2}
\right).
$$

The unit-normalized cousin \(T_a(x)=2E_a(x)\) gives

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty T_a(x)T_b(x)\,dx
=
\pi
\left(
\frac1a-\frac{b}{3a^2}
\right),
\qquad a\geq b.
}
$$

Notice the contrast.

For two normalized sine fractions, if \(a\geq b\), the answer is independent of \(b\).

For two cosine-difference cousins, if \(a\geq b\), the answer still depends on \(b\).

That contrast will matter much later.

```{ojs}
viewof a_cc = Inputs.range([0.5, 6], {
  value: 3,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof b_cc = Inputs.range([0.1, 6], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "b"
})
```

```{ojs}
m_cc = Math.min(a_cc, b_cc)
M_cc = Math.max(a_cc, b_cc)

exactCC_half = Math.PI * m_cc * m_cc * (3 * M_cc - m_cc) / (12 * a_cc * a_cc * b_cc * b_cc)
exactCC_unit = 4 * exactCC_half

approxCC_unit = trapIntegral(
  x => cosDiffUnit(x, a_cc) * cosDiffUnit(x, b_cc),
  100,
  7000
)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.6;">
  Exact value for <code>E_a E_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(exactCC_half)}</b><br>
  Exact value for <code>T_a T_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(exactCC_unit)}</b><br>
  Numerical approximation for <code>T_a T_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(approxCC_unit)}</b>
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
ccData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 50 / 999;
  return {
    x,
    y: cosDiffUnit(x, a_cc) * cosDiffUnit(x, b_cc)
  };
})

Plot.line(ccData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "T_a(x) T_b(x)"},
  grid: true
})
```

The formula is no longer a perfect plateau, but it is still governed by the relative sizes of \(a\) and \(b\).

---

## Mixing a normalized sine fraction with a cosine-difference cousin

The next natural experiment is mixed:

$$
L(a,b)=
\int_0^\infty
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
\frac{1-\cos(bx)}{(bx)^2}
\,dx.
$$

The answer is

$$
\boxed{
L(a,b)
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\pi}{4a}, & a\geq b,\\[1em]
\dfrac{\pi}{2b}-\dfrac{\pi a}{4b^2}, & 0<a<b.
\end{cases}
}
$$

If we use the unit-normalized cousin

$$
T_b(x)=\frac{2(1-\cos(bx))}{(bx)^2},
$$

then

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}T_b(x)\,dx
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\pi}{2a}, & a\geq b,\\[1em]
\dfrac{\pi}{b}-\dfrac{\pi a}{2b^2}, & 0<a<b.
\end{cases}
}
$$

This mixed case is important because it shows that the cosine-difference cousin can be folded into the same product story.

```{ojs}
viewof a_mix = Inputs.range([0.5, 6], {
  value: 3,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof b_mix = Inputs.range([0.1, 6], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "b"
})
```

```{ojs}
exactMix_half = a_mix >= b_mix
  ? Math.PI / (4 * a_mix)
  : Math.PI / (2 * b_mix) - Math.PI * a_mix / (4 * b_mix * b_mix)

exactMix_unit = 2 * exactMix_half

approxMix_unit = trapIntegral(
  x => sineFrac(x, a_mix) * cosDiffUnit(x, b_mix),
  120,
  8000
)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.6;">
  Exact value for <code>S_a E_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(exactMix_half)}</b><br>
  Exact value for <code>S_a T_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(exactMix_unit)}</b><br>
  Numerical approximation for <code>S_a T_b</code>:
  <b>${fmt(approxMix_unit)}</b>
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
mixData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 60 / 999;
  return {
    x,
    y: sineFrac(x, a_mix) * cosDiffUnit(x, b_mix)
  };
})

Plot.line(mixData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "S_a(x) T_b(x)"},
  grid: true
})
```

Again, when the sine fraction has the larger scale, it creates a plateau.

That is not an accident. In Act II, this will become a random-walk “safe window” statement.

---

## Powers of the normalized sine fraction

After products of two factors, the next question is obvious:

What happens if we multiply many copies?

For example,

$$
\int_0^\infty
\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^n
\,dx.
$$

The first few cases already show that something interesting is happening:

$$
\int_0^\infty \frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2},
$$

$$
\int_0^\infty \left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2 dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2},
$$

but

$$
\int_0^\infty \left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^3 dx
=
\frac{3\pi}{8}.
$$

So powers do not stay fixed forever.

This repeated-factor case is a special version of the more general product

$$
\int_0^\infty
\prod_{k=0}^{n}
\frac{\sin(a_kx)}{a_kx}
\,dx.
$$

That is the main object of Act II.

---

## Powers of the cosine-difference cousin

The same question can be asked for

$$
E_a(x)=\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}.
$$

But here the identity from earlier is decisive:

$$
E_a(x)
=
\frac12
\left(
\frac{\sin\left(\frac{a x}{2}\right)}
{\frac{a x}{2}}
\right)^2.
$$

Therefore

$$
E_a(x)^n
=
\frac{1}{2^n}
\left(
\frac{\sin\left(\frac{a x}{2}\right)}
{\frac{a x}{2}}
\right)^{2n}.
$$

So powers of the cosine-difference cousin are secretly even powers of the normalized sine fraction at half scale.

This is the main lesson of Act I:

> We started with several different-looking fractions. But as soon as we began multiplying them, they kept leading back to the same product problem.

---

## The bridge to Act II

Let us summarize the path.

We began with one wave:

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}.
$$

Then we multiplied two such waves and found

$$
\int_0^\infty
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
\frac{\sin(bx)}{bx}
\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2\max(a,b)}.
$$

Then we studied the cousin

$$
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2},
$$

and discovered that it is secretly built from the same normalized sine fraction.

Then we mixed the two families and raised them to powers.

Again and again, the same question returned:

$$
\int_0^\infty
\prod_{k=0}^{n}
\frac{\sin(a_kx)}{a_kx}
\,dx.
$$

This is where the real mystery begins.

In Act II, we will see why this integral sometimes refuses to change even after we multiply by many extra factors, why the famous pattern eventually breaks, and why the explanation involves random walkers, safe windows, and sliced high-dimensional cubes.
